blocks.text: Then Tobias remembered the words of Raphael, and he took the fish's liver and heart out of the bag where he had them and put them on the embers of the incense.
The odor of the fish so repelled the demon that he fled to the remotest parts of Egypt. But Raphael followed him, and at once bound him there hand and foot.
3 : , see 6.8n. , binding restrains demons (see 10.4).
When the parents had gone out and shut the door of the room, Tobias got out of bed and said to Sarah,a “Sister, get up, and let us pray and implore our Lord that he grant us mercy and safety.”

So she got up, and they began to pray and implore that they might be kept safe. Tobias began by saying, “Blessed are you, O God of our ancestors, and blessed is your name in all generations forever. Let the heavens and the whole creation bless you forever.

4–5 : The couple's praying contrasts with both Raguel's grave-digging (8.9–11) and descriptions of ardor found in first and second century non-Jewish Hellenistic romances (e.g., Chariton, ; Longus, ). , see 5.21n.
5 : , see 3.11n. , see Ex 3.13; Deut 4.1.

You made Adam, and for him you made his wife Eve as a helper and support. From the two of them the human race has sprung. You said, ‘It is not good that the man should be alone; let us make a helper for him like himself.’
6 : and , Gen 2.18–23; one of the earliest references to both outside of Genesis. , an addition to Gen 2.18; cf. 2.13n. , introducing a quotation of Gen 2.18.
I now am taking this kinswoman of mine, not because of lust, but with sincerity. Grant that she and I may find mercy and that we may grow old together.”
5–7 : Prayers are frequently attributed to characters in Second Temple Jewish literature (e.g., Song of Thr; 2 Macc 1.24–29; see also 3.11n.).
7 : , chastity is a popular theme in Jewish-Hellenistic literature; see also 3.14–15n.
And they both said, “Amen, Amen.”

8 : The text, discreetly, does not note when the marriage was consummated. The Vulgate, reflecting Jerome's interest in celibacy, adds that the couple abstained for three nights before consummating their marriage. , Sarah's only words aside from her prayer (3.11–15).
Then they went to sleep for the night. But Raguel arose and called his servants to him, and they went and dug a grave,
8.1–9a: Asmodeus is exorcised.
for he said, “It is possible that he will die and we will become an object of ridicule and derision.”
9–10 : Raguel's morbid planning contrasts with Tobias's piety; unlike Tobit, Raguel prepares a grave before finding a corpse.
10 : , Raguel fears for his reputation, not his daughter's happiness or his son-in-law's life; Sarah had already become an object of derision (3.7–9).
When they had finished digging the grave, Raguel went into his house and called his wife,
saying, “Send one of the maids and have her go in to see if he is alive. But if he is dead, let us bury him without anyone knowing it.”
12 : ignores Azariah/Raphael, the servants, and the concerns of Tobit and Anna.
So they sent the maid, lit a lamp, and opened the door; and she went in and found them sound asleep together.
Then the maid came out and informed them that he was alive and that nothing was wrong.
So they blessed the God of heaven, and Raguel said, “Blessed are you, O God, with every pure blessing; let all your chosen ones bless you. Let them bless you forever.
Blessed are you because you have made me glad. It has not turned out as I expected, but you have dealt with us according to your great mercy.
Blessed are you because you had compassion on two only children. Be merciful to them, O Master, and keep them safe; bring their lives to fulfillment in happiness and mercy.”
15–17 : The prayer continues Raguel's wry perspective: he praises God for his own good fortune before mentioning the mercy extended to the couple.
Then he ordered his servants to fill in the grave before daybreak.
8.9b–18: Raguel's fears are assuaged.
After this he asked his wife to bake many loaves of bread; and he went out to the herd and brought two steers and four rams and ordered them to be slaughtered. So they began to make preparations.

Then he called for Tobias and swore on oath to him in these words: “You shall not leave here for fourteen days, but shall stay here eating and drinking with me; and you shall cheer up my daughter, who has been depressed.
8.20 : , twice as long as a usual wedding celebration (cf. 11.18; Judg 14.12). either because of past deaths or the prospect of leaving her home.

Take at once half of what I own and return in safety to your father; the other half will be yours when my wife and I die. Take courage, my child. I am your father and Edna is your mother, and we belong to you as well as to your wife now and forever. Take courage, my child.”
8.19–9.6: The wedding celebrated and tasks fulfilled.
21 : is what Edna advised Sarah (7.16), but under much different circumstances. and together with promises of inheritance suggest an adoption formula (see 10.12).
